words.definition: the joining of two companies into one
example_sentences: The merger created the largest bank in the country.
The two airlines announced a merger last week.
Shareholders voted in favour of the merger.
words.pronunciation_tip: MUR-jer — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse merger (two companies joining voluntarily) with acquisition (one buying another).

words.definition: the total income generated by a business
example_sentences: The company reported record revenue last quarter.
Revenue fell by 15% due to the economic downturn.
Marketing aims to increase both revenue and profit.
words.pronunciation_tip: REV-uh-nyoo — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse revenue (total income) with profit (income minus costs).

words.definition: anyone who has an interest in or is affected by a business
example_sentences: All stakeholders were consulted before the decision.
Employees, customers, and investors are all stakeholders.
The report was distributed to key stakeholders.
words.pronunciation_tip: STAYK-hohl-der — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the people involved' — say 'stakeholders' in business contexts.

words.definition: a debt or legal responsibility; the opposite of an asset
example_sentences: The company's liabilities exceed its assets.
Personal liability is limited in a limited company.
Being sued created a significant liability for the business.
words.pronunciation_tip: ly-uh-BIL-ih-tee — five syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse liability (financial) with liability (legal responsibility) — both are used.

words.definition: the purchase of one company by another
example_sentences: The tech giant announced the acquisition of a startup.
The acquisition cost 2 billion dollars.
Through acquisitions, the company tripled in size.
words.pronunciation_tip: ak-wih-ZIH-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'they bought the company' — say 'they made an acquisition'.

words.definition: a payment made to shareholders from company profits
example_sentences: The company declared a dividend of 50p per share.
Dividends attract investors looking for regular income.
They cut the dividend due to poor results.
words.pronunciation_tip: DIV-ih-dend — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'payment to shareholders' every time — say 'dividend'.

words.definition: to plan and control spending
example_sentences: We need to budget carefully for the new product launch.
She budgets every department's expenses each quarter.
The project was carefully budgeted at 500,000 euros.
words.pronunciation_tip: BUJ-it — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'plan the money' — say 'budget for' or 'budget carefully'.

words.definition: the movement of money into and out of a business
example_sentences: Poor cash flow is the main reason small businesses fail.
They had strong profits but a serious cash flow problem.
Managing cash flow is crucial in the early years.
words.pronunciation_tip: KASH-floh — two words, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse cash flow (when money moves) with profit (how much is made).
words.collocations: ['manage cash flow', 'cash flow problem', 'positive cash flow']

words.definition: a prediction of future financial or business performance
example_sentences: The sales forecast for the next quarter is positive.
Analysts forecast a recovery in the second half.
She forecasted revenues of 10 million for the year.
words.pronunciation_tip: FAWR-kast — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'prediction of the business results' — say 'forecast'.

words.definition: ongoing business costs not directly linked to production
example_sentences: Rent and utilities are significant overheads.
Reducing overheads was the priority during the recession.
High overheads make it difficult to stay competitive.
words.pronunciation_tip: OH-ver-hed — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'costs of running the business' every time — say 'overheads'.

words.definition: a persuasive presentation to potential clients or investors
example_sentences: The start-up team delivered a brilliant pitch.
She pitched the idea to the board of directors.
His pitch won them a contract worth 2 million.
words.pronunciation_tip: PICH — one syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I presented to investors' casually — say 'I pitched to investors'.

words.definition: the final profit or loss of a business; the most important point
example_sentences: The acquisition will improve the company's bottom line.
Let me cut to the bottom line: we need to reduce costs.
The bottom line is that we cannot afford to continue.
words.pronunciation_tip: BOT-um LYN.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the final profit' always — 'bottom line' can also mean 'the key point'.
words.collocations: ['improve the bottom line', 'bottom line impact', 'cut to the bottom line']

words.definition: a standard used to compare performance
example_sentences: We benchmark our prices against the competition.
The new results are well above industry benchmarks.
Setting clear benchmarks helps measure success.
words.pronunciation_tip: BENCH-mark — stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a standard comparison' every time — say 'benchmark'.

words.definition: using borrowed money or resources to increase potential returns
example_sentences: The company used leverage to fund its expansion.
She leveraged her network to get the contract.
High leverage increases both risk and reward.
words.pronunciation_tip: LEV-er-ij — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'borrowed money to invest' every time — say 'leverage'.

words.definition: the ongoing fixed costs of running a business
example_sentences: Their overheads doubled when they moved to a bigger office.
Reducing overheads allowed the company to lower prices.
High overheads are a major challenge for small firms.
words.pronunciation_tip: OH-ver-hed — stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'fixed costs' and 'overheads' as completely different — overheads ARE fixed costs.

words.definition: the ability of a business or system to grow without major new investment
example_sentences: Scalability is key for any digital business model.
The app's scalability means it can handle millions of users.
Investors look for scalability when evaluating startups.
words.pronunciation_tip: skay-luh-BIL-ih-tee — five syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'can grow easily' as a noun — say 'scalability'.

words.definition: the extent to which customers recognise a brand
example_sentences: The campaign dramatically increased brand awareness.
Brand awareness is the first step in the sales funnel.
Low brand awareness makes it hard to compete.
words.pronunciation_tip: BRAND uh-WAIR-ness.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'people know our brand' as a noun — say 'brand awareness'.
words.collocations: ['increase brand awareness', 'high brand awareness', 'brand awareness campaign']

words.definition: thorough research done before making a business decision
example_sentences: The company conducted due diligence before the acquisition.
Proper due diligence takes several months.
Skipping due diligence is a costly mistake.
words.pronunciation_tip: DYOO DIL-ih-junss.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'checking everything carefully' — say 'conducting due diligence'.
words.collocations: ['conduct due diligence', 'due diligence process', 'complete due diligence']

words.definition: a person who owns shares in a company
example_sentences: Shareholders voted to approve the new strategy.
Major shareholders can influence company decisions.
The dividends were paid to all shareholders.
words.pronunciation_tip: SHAIR-hohl-der — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the owners of shares' every time — say 'shareholders'.

words.definition: a change from failure to success, or the time to complete a task
example_sentences: The company made an impressive turnaround in just two years.
She was hired specifically to lead the turnaround.
The turnaround time for orders is 48 hours.
words.pronunciation_tip: TUR-nuh-rownd — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'change from bad to good' — say 'a turnaround' or 'a business turnaround'.
